Chapter 7 or any other provisions of the Political Reform 
Act of 1974 will be necessary under the following specific 
circumstances: 
1.

sections.question: 1. A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose was to testify before a committee of the State Legislature as a representative of the City of San Jose, authorized to appear before that committee by the City Council. The subject of the testimony was a bill being considered by the legislature which would directly affect the revenues of the City of San Jose. It develops that because of the fact that there are so few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and the fact that the Councilmember has a busy schedule, the only way that they can testify is by flying a private non-commercial airplane. If this flight was arranged by a private person who owned the plane, would the Councilmember be required to report this plane ride as a gift in their annual statement filed pursuant to Article 2, Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act? 2. As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose in litigation affecting the City. Because of my schedule, I will be unable to make a timely appearance and will need to use a private plane to arrive on time for the oral argument. Would I be required to report this benefit under Chapter 7 of the Government Code or Article 7 of the Fair Political Practices Act?
sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney of San Jose seeks advice on whether a City Council member and the City Attorney must report private airplane flights provided to enable their official representation of the City before the State Legislature and State Supreme Court, respectively, as gifts under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both August 25, 1976 initial request and November 18, 1976 follow-up inquiry. This is an incoming request, not an FPPC response. Some text is obscured/unclear due to document quality, but the main questions are identifiable. A partial FPPC response draft appears in the document but is incomplete and fragmentary.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1. A member of the City Council of the City of San Jose was to testify before a committee of the State Legislature as a representative of the City of San Jose, authorized to appear before that committee by the City Council. The subject of the testimony was a bill being considered by the legislature which would directly affect the revenues of the City of San Jose. It develops that because of the fact that there are so few flights between San Jose and Sacramento and the fact that the Councilmember has a busy schedule, the only way that they can testify is by flying a private non-commercial airplane. If this flight was arranged by a private person who owned the plane, would the Councilmember be required to report this plane ride as a gift in their annual statement filed pursuant to Article 2, Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act? 2. As City Attorney, I am required to appear before the State Supreme Court representing the City of San Jose in litigation affecting the City. Because of my schedule, I will be unable to make a timely appearance and will need to use a private plane to arrive on time for the oral argument. Would I be required to report this benefit under Chapter 7 of the Government Code or Article 7 of the Fair Political Practices Act?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from Peter G. Stone, City Attorney of San Jose, to the FPPC Technical Assistance and Analysis Division seeking advice on reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act for private airplane flights arranged for city officials performing official duties.
